# UC-22.2.18 · NIS2 Network Security Monitoring and Anomaly Detection

## Description

Flags hour-bucketed network behaviour that looks like wide port sweeps, brute-force blocks, or very large flows against the same peers, using accelerated CIM Network_Traffic summaries plus simple thresholds so unusual sequences stand out without waiting for the ticket queue to notice them.

## Value

Because regulators and boards care whether protective measures actually run day to day, this gives security leadership a defensible trail that someone is watching the wire for denial-of-service precursors, probing, and east-west oddities—not only that a policy PDF exists on the intranet.

## Implementation

(1) Install Splunk Enterprise Security (263) and Palo Alto / Cisco ASA add-ons (2757, 1620); confirm firewall and IDS data land in CIM-tagged indexes. (2) Accelerate the Network_Traffic data model (Settings → Data models → Network_Traffic, 30-day summary window typical). (3) Populate `asset_lookup_by_str` so unknown-internal sources are rare. (4) Save the SPL as scheduled alert `UC-22.2.18`, 60-minute window, cron every hour, throttle by `src`+`dest` for 4 hours. (5) Route ES notable events or alert actions to your SOC channel with the `risk_signal` field in the title.

## Detailed Implementation

Prerequisites
• Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263) on the search tier with a role that can run accelerated `tstats` against Network_Traffic and read `index=network` / `index=ids`.
• Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (Splunkbase 2757) and Cisco ASA add-on (Splunkbase 1620) installed on search heads plus indexers or heavy forwarders doing parsing.
• Firewall data ingested with sourcetypes such as `sourcetype=pan:traffic` and `sourcetype=cisco:asa`; IDS events as `sourcetype=snort` or `suricata` inside `index=ids` (adjust names to your deployment).
• Network_Traffic data model acceleration enabled for at least 30 days of history; plan roughly 5–15% of firewall ingest volume for summary storage.
• `asset_lookup_by_str` maintained under Enterprise Security → Content → Assets for tying RFC1918 sources to owning teams.
• RBAC: grant `ess_analyst` or custom role `nis2_netsec_read` rights to `network`, `ids`, and summary indexes; avoid sharing admin tokens.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Point Palo Alto firewall syslog or streaming feeds at Splunk heavy forwarders with `props.conf` from add-on 2757 so fields `src`, `dest`, `action`, `bytes`, `dest_port`, and `app` extract consistently. For Cisco ASA, enable `logging timestamp` and send to the same tier with `sourcetype=cisco:asa` tagging.

For IDS, forward unified alerts with 5-minute **forwarder** checkpoints; typical volume is far smaller than firewall permit logs—keep a dedicated `index=ids` with 90-day hot retention.

In ES, run Content Management → Deploy the Network_Traffic tags (CIM version aligned to your ES release). Validate with a 15-minute exploratory search on `index=network sourcetype=pan:traffic` comparing event counts to the firewall vendor UI (Monitor → Traffic logs) for the same interval.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Use the UC SPL block (saved as `UC-22.2.18`). Understanding the pipeline: `tstats` against `summariesonly` reads accelerated Network_Traffic summaries—if this returns zero while raw firewall events exist, acceleration is off or tags missing. The `where` clause drops known DNS/NTP heavy hitters to reduce baseline noise. Thresholds (`dest_ports > 20`, blocked `count > 100`, `total_bytes > 10GB`) are starting points; tighten per segment after **72 hours** of baseline tuning.

Schedule the alert hourly with earliest `-65m@m` and latest `@m` so each run spans full clock hours. Throttle by `src` and `dest` for **240 minutes**.

Step 3 — Validate
Pick a controlled Qualys scan window: you should see elevated `dest_ports` with `risk_signal` “port_scan” unless your scanner subnet is allow-listed—confirm suppression works. For a known backup job, verify `total_bytes` spikes without alert when the `nis2_uc2218_noise_allowlist.csv` lookup marks that subnet.

Spot-check five random alert rows against the Palo Alto Traffic Monitor at the same `_time` hour; bytes totals should match within ingestion lag (<300 **seconds** typical).

Step 4 — Operationalize
Publish an ES correlation search or native alert feeding a “NIS2 — Network anomaly” dashboard: row of single values for counts by `risk_signal`, timechart of blocked brute-force attempts, and drilldown table keyed on `src`→`dest`.

Add a workflow action opening ServiceNow SecOps with prefilled `risk_signal` and CMDB lookup from `asset_lookup_by_str`.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **Zero tstats results** — open Settings → Data Models → Network_Traffic → Acceleration; verify summary builds at 100%. Check that events carry CIM tags: `index=network | tags output=all | head 1`.
• **All sources “unknown_asset”** — refresh ES asset merge or verify `asset_lookup_by_str` key format matches `src` field.
• **Noise from NAT gateways** — collapse behind NAT using `nat_translation_lookup.csv`; enrich before the `lookup asset_lookup_by_str` stage.
• **IDS skew** — if Snort timestamps drift >60 **seconds** from firewall, align NTP on sensors and forwarders; align `_time` using `eval _time=strptime(...)` only if vendor JSON lacks standard time.
• **Performance** — narrow with `where All_Traffic.action!="allowed"` during DDoS storms to keep search under **30 seconds** completion SLA.

## SPL

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` count sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as total_bytes dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as dest_ports
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  where NOT All_Traffic.dest_category IN ("internal_server","dns","ntp")
  by All_Traffic.src All_Traffic.dest All_Traffic.action span=1h
| rename All_Traffic.* as *
| where dest_ports > 20 OR (action="blocked" AND count > 100) OR total_bytes > 10737418240
| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS src OUTPUT category as src_category, priority as src_priority
| eval risk_signal=case(
    dest_ports > 50, "port_scan",
    action="blocked" AND count > 500, "brute_force",
    total_bytes > 10737418240, "data_exfiltration",
    isnull(src_category), "unknown_asset",
    1=1, "anomaly")
| sort - count
| table _time, src, dest, src_category, risk_signal, dest_ports, count, total_bytes, action
```

## CIM SPL

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
```

## Visualization

Table (anomalies), Bar chart (risk signals by type), Map (traffic flows), Timeline (blocked connections), Sankey diagram (source to destination flows).
